title: Records, drift, and the longest increasing subsequence of biased Gaussian random walks
abstract: The longest increasing subsequence (LIS) of a random walk has so far been studied mainly for zero-mean, symmetric step increments. We numerically investigate the LIS of biased Gaussian random walks, with unit-variance increments and positive drift $μ_{p} = Φ^{-1}(p)$, where $p = \mathbb{P}(ξ>0)$. In contrast with the symmetric case, we find that for every fixed $p>1/2$ the mean LIS length grows linearly, $\langle L_{n}(p)\rangle \sim a(p)n$, with $a(p)$ increasing from $0$ at $p=1/2$ to $1$ as $p \to 1$. The record count is also linear, with coefficient $λ(p)$ given by Spitzer's formula for the mean ascending ladder epoch, and the LIS becomes increasingly aligned with this record skeleton as $p$ grows. At the symmetric point $p=1/2$, the record skeleton collapses to the Sparre Andersen $\sqrt{n}$ scale, while the LIS returns to the symmetric finite-variance $\sqrt{n}\log{n}$ regime. Near this limit, the excess $a(μ_{p})-λ(μ_{p})$ vanishes more slowly than linearly in the drift, although our data do not resolve a single power law. The empirical distribution of $L_{n}$ also changes across the singular point, from lognormal-like at $p=1/2$ to fluctuations consistent with Gaussian behavior for every sampled $p>1/2$.
summary: This work analyzes the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) of a biased Gaussian random walk, finding that for positive drift, both the LIS length and the record count grow linearly with time. The paper highlights a sharp transition in statistical behavior at the symmetric point ($p=1/2$), where the LIS distribution changes from lognormal-like to Gaussian, providing deep insights into how drift affects extreme path statistics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the growth rate and mechanism of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) for a biased Gaussian random walk, contrasting it with the well-studied symmetric case.
main_result: For positive drift ($p>1/2$), the mean LIS length grows linearly ($\langle L_n(p) angle \sim a(p)n$), while the record count also grows linearly ($\langle R_n(p) angle \sim \lambda(p)n$).
method: The analysis combines numerical simulations of the random walk with asymptotic analysis, comparing the LIS growth to established results for record processes.
model_or_system: The model is a biased Gaussian random walk $X_k = X_{k-1} + \xi_k$, where the bias is controlled by $p = \mathbb{P}(\xi>0)$, leading to a drift $\mu_p$.
key_observables: Mean LIS length ($\langle L_n(p) angle$), mean record count ($\langle R_n(p) angle$), and the fluctuation excess $a(p) - \lambda(p)$.
important_parameters: The bias parameter $p$, the symmetric point $p=1/2$, and the drift $\mu_p$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the existence of linear asymptotic growth rates for both LIS and record counts when $p>1/2$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the rapid transition of the effective exponent $	heta_{eff}$ to 1 for $p>1/2$, plot the linear coefficients $a(p)$ and $\lambda(p)$ vs. $p$, and show the distribution changes from lognormal-like at $p=1/2$ to Gaussian for $p>1/2$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem by contrasting biased vs. symmetric random walks. It derives the linear scaling for $p>1/2$, analyzes the record process using Spitzer's formula, and then examines the singular behavior near the symmetric point $p=1/2$, concluding with a discussion of the non-record contribution.
why_interesting: While not directly quantum, the study of extreme statistics (like LIS) in random processes is foundational to understanding fluctuations in complex systems, which has analogues in quantum measurement statistics or disordered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Gaussian increment density φ(x|µ,σ2) with σ2 = 1 and µ = µp = Φ−1(p), so that P(ξ > 0) = p ≥1/2. The shaded area has mass p−1/2 relative to the centered case µ = 0.

FIG. 2. Effective exponent θeff(p,n) = ∆log⟨Ln⟩/∆logn, evaluated at the geometric midpoint of consecutive walk lengths. For every sampled p > 1/2, the curves rapidly approach θ = 1. The symmetric case p = 1/2 remains below unity, consistent with the finite-variance √n logn regime.

FIG. 3. Linear coefficients a(p) = ⟨Ln⟩/n and λ(p) = ⟨Rn⟩/n for the biased Gaussian random walk at n = 107, with 104 samples. The red dashed curve is the theoretical prediction (24), and the gray dotted line is the high-drift reference a = p. At p = 1/2, the plotted points are finite-n ratios; the limiting linear coefficients vanish. Standard errors are smaller than the markers.

FIG. 5. Mechanism diagnostic ratios RLIS n /Ln and RLIS n /Rn for the biased Gaussian random walk at n = 107. The ratios are small at p = 1/2 and approach unity as p →1. Their separation in the intermediate range reflects the non-record contribution to the LIS.

FIG. 4. Quantile–quantile plot of the standardized LIS length (Ln − ⟨Ln⟩)/sd(Ln) at n = 106 against the standard Gaussian quantile. At p = 1/2, the distribution is right-skewed and fat-tailed, consistent with the lognormal-like bulk reported for symmetric walks. For the sampled p > 1/2, the points follow the Gaussian reference line within Monte Carlo scatter.
